ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn provides a generic beef rib food product entry; no specific entry exists for braised beef ribs in black pepper sauce. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked bovine rib meat; no sub-code exists for the black pepper sauce preparation. Sauce components are not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for braised beef ribs in black pepper sauce as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of beef ribs in black pepper sauce is approximately 250 g, typically presented as a plate of 4–6 bone-in rib pieces at a dim sum restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, ribs, short, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised (NDB 13352); supplemented with HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data for Chinese braised beef dishes
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (protein, fat, energy) anchored to USDA FoodData Central entry for braised beef short ribs (FDC ID 174032 / NDB 13352). Sodium and sauce-related values (sodium 480 mg/100g, sugar 2.0 g/100g, carbohydrate 6.0 g/100g) are elevated relative to plain braised ribs due to soy sauce, oyster sauce, and black pepper sauce; these values are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo data for Chinese braised beef dishes and are retained from the input record with medium confidence. Saturated fat 6.0 g/100g and cholesterol 48 mg/100g are consistent with USDA data for braised beef ribs. Trans fat 0.2 g/100g is consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in ruminant beef as reported by USDA.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on mixed meat dish with starch-thickened sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef ribs in black pepper sauce. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on: (1) the dominant macronutrient is protein and fat, which have negligible glycaemic effect; (2) carbohydrate content (6.0 g/100g) derives primarily from corn starch coating and sauce sugars, which are partially offset by the fat and protein matrix slowing gastric emptying; (3) analogous mixed meat dishes with starch-thickened sauces in the University of Sydney GI Database cluster in the GI 30–45 range. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 15.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.3 (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg/100g; a 250 g serving delivers 1200 mg sodium — approximately 52% of the WHO 2300 mg daily limit — driven by soy sauce, oyster sauce, and black pepper sauce.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g, request sauce served separately, and ask for reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to bring sodium intake below 600 mg per meal occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 6.0 g/100g with low fibre (0.4 g/100g) and hidden sugars from oyster sauce; high fat content (15.2 g/100g) may contribute to insulin resistance with regular consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the portion to 100–150 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre and moderate postprandial glucose, and request corn starch coating be reduced and sauce served on the side to control glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100g and sodium 480 mg/100g; a 250 g serving provides 15 g saturated fat (75% of the 20 g daily limit) and 1200 mg sodium, both of which are established cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional food, limit servings to 100 g or less, choose leaner cuts where available, and avoid consuming the sauce to reduce both saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat high in arachidonic acid (omega-6) and saturated fat 6.0 g/100g amplify pro-inflammatory pathways; sodium 480 mg/100g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise red meat consumption; if consumed, limit to a small portion (80–100 g) and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as leafy greens or omega-3-rich fish at other meals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Ribs in Black Pepper Sauce (黑椒牛肋骨) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean dish commonly served at dim sum restaurants and zi char stalls. The dish consists of bone-in beef short ribs that are first marinated in soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and ginger, then braised or steamed until tender, and finally coated in a rich, aromatic black pepper sauce thickened with corn starch. The result is deeply savoury, mildly spicy from the cracked black pepper, and slightly sweet from the oyster sauce, with a glossy, clingy sauce that coats each rib. The texture is tender and gelatinous near the bone, with a satisfying chew. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 220 kcal/100g, with moderate protein (14 g/100g) and high fat (15.2 g/100g), of which 6.0 g/100g is saturated fat. Sodium is high at 480 mg/100g due to multiple condiments. Carbohydrate content is modest at 6.0 g/100g, primarily from the corn starch coating and sauce sugars, giving a low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 35. Clinically, this dish is a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat load, and a moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to hidden sugars and low fibre. It is strongly pro-inflammatory for rheumatoid arthritis due to its red meat and arachidonic acid content. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 250 g (one plate), delivering around 550 kcal, 1200 mg sodium, and 15 g saturated fat.

confidence_reason: Core macros are anchored to USDA FoodData Central data for braised beef short ribs, which is an approved primary source and a close match. Sodium and carbohydrate values are elevated relative to plain braised ribs due to sauce components; these are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo data for Chinese braised beef dishes and are retained with medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. No exact entry for this specific dish exists in any single approved source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for braised beef ribs in black pepper sauce (as a composite dish with sauce) is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish preparation are not available in a single approved source entry as of 2026-04-16; USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174032) was consulted but does not report all micronutrients for the sauce-coated preparation. trans_fat_g 0.2 g/100g reflects naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid trans fats present in ruminant beef, consistent with USDA data; this is not industrially produced trans fat. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database cluster data for mixed meat dishes with starch-thickened sauces; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found. Halal status is marked false as beef ribs prepared with oyster sauce and soy sauce from unverified sources cannot be assumed halal without certification.
